summary: Escaped slave who became the most prominent Black abolitionist orator, writer, and statesman of 19th-century America. Fled slavery in Maryland in 1838 and wrote three autobiographies, most famously the 1845 Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave, describing his climactic fistfight with the 'slave-breaker' Edward Covey. Founded and edited the abolitionist newspaper The North Star and delivered landmark speeches including 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' Regarded as the most photographed American of the 19th century, he held Reconstruction-era political posts including president of the Freedman's Savings Bank and U.S. Minister to Haiti, and ran as Victoria Woodhull's vice-presidential running mate in 1872.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: An escaped slave who became the leading Black abolitionist voice of 19th-century America, known for his oratory, his three autobiographies, and his newspaper The North Star. He is most often identified as the most photographed American of the 19th century and through his authorship of My Bondage and My Freedom.

clues.clue: Escaped slave who became a prominent abolitionist orator, writer, and statesman; founded and edited the newspaper The North Star and wrote autobiographies including the 1845 Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass and 1855 My Bondage and My Freedom
examples: name this creator of The North Star, whose autobiographies include My Bondage and My Freedom.
name this character, a prominent abolitionist who relates the narrative of his life of slavery in a notable autobiography.

clues.clue: Regarded as the most photographed American of the 19th century
examples: John Stauffer collaborated on an illustrated biography titled "Picturing" this man, the most photographed American of the 19th century.
name this most photographed American of the 19th century, who used the newspaper The North Star to advocate for African-American rights.

works.name: Enslavement and Escape
works.description: Born enslaved in Maryland, he taught himself to read against his enslavers' wishes, fought back against a hired 'slave-breaker,' and escaped north to freedom in 1838, settling in Massachusetts.

clues.clue: As a child, he was given a supposed magical root by a man named Sandy, meant to ward off harm if kept on the right side of his body
examples: This figure is given a supposed magical root by a man named Sandy.
Prior to this scene, Sandy gives the narrator a "root" that will ward off harm if it's kept on the right side of his body.

clues.clue: As a child, he befriended a boy named Daniel and tricked older white boys into giving him reading lessons by pretending to already know more than them
examples: As a child he befriends Daniel and tricks older boys into giving him reading lessons by pretending to know more than them.

clues.clue: He recalled witnessing the overseer Mr. Gore shoot the enslaved man Demby, who had refused to leave a stream
examples: He remembers an incident in which Demby refuses to leave a stream and is shot by Mr. Gore.

clues.clue: He learned to read partly thanks to Sophia Auld, before her husband Hugh Auld forbade it; he was later transformed after reading a copy of The Columbian Orator
examples: This man recalled how he was almost "broken" by Edward Covey and learned to read thanks to Sophie Auld.
The protagonist of a work by this author learns how to read from the Columbian Orator after being forbidden by Hugh Auld.

clues.clue: He physically fought and defeated the sadistic 'slave-breaker' Edward Covey, to whom he had been leased, in a climactic two-hour fight in a horse stable; the incident, in which he addressed passing ships as if they were free, permanently ended Covey's abuse of him
examples: He recalls speaking to the ships that sail by before relating his fight with the sadistic Edward Covey.
This scene unfolds over two hours in a horse stable, and results in a sadistic man nicknamed "the snake" never harming the protagonist again.

clues.clue: He worked as a caulker at a Baltimore shipyard while enslaved to Hugh and Thomas Auld, forced to turn over all his wages
examples: He becomes a caulker at a Baltimore shipyard, but is forced to turn all his wages over to Hugh Auld.
This orator who ran the abolitionist newspaper The North Star also wrote about his experiences working in a shipyard and as a caulker in Baltimore while serving Thomas Auld.

clues.clue: After escaping from Maryland in 1838, he married Anna Murray and settled in New Bedford, Massachusetts, where he was given food by Nathan Johnson
examples: He marries Anna Murray and goes to New Bedford, Massachusetts, after escaping from Maryland.
recalled in one book being given food by Nathan Johnson.

works.name: Autobiographies
works.description: Douglass wrote three increasingly detailed autobiographies over his lifetime, beginning with the 1845 Narrative that made him famous and ending with a memoir covering his Reconstruction-era political career.

clues.clue: Published the Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave in 1845 while living in Lynn, Massachusetts, with a foreword written by the abolitionist William Lloyd Garrison
examples: William Lloyd Garrison wrote an introduction to this author's memoir, which depicts his fight against Edward Covey.
This freed slave wrote and published a namesake 1845 Narrative of his Life while living in Lynn, Massachusetts.

clues.clue: In the expanded 1855 rewrite My Bondage and My Freedom, he added a new character, Caroline, who milks a cow, to the fight scene from the earlier Narrative
examples: This author added a character named Caroline who goes to milk a cow in a rewritten version of a scene that appears in an earlier work which lasts for "nearly two hours."

clues.clue: He wrote about escaping his enslavement across three autobiographies
examples: name this author who wrote about escaping his enslavement in three autobiographies including My Bondage and My Freedom and a Narrative in the Life of himself, An American Slave.

clues.clue: He described ships sailing by that seemed like 'shrouded ghosts,' 'robed in purest white,' shortly before recounting his fight with Covey
examples: This author described ships "robed in purest white" that seemed like "shrouded ghosts" in a work in which Sandy gives the protagonist a root to keep on the right side of his body to ward off harm.

works.name: Newspapers and Publications
works.description: Douglass built and ran a series of abolitionist and Reconstruction-era newspapers over four decades, using them as his primary public platform.

clues.clue: Founded and edited The North Star, an abolitionist newspaper based in Rochester, New York, beginning in 1847; its office was located near that of fellow narrative-writer Harriet Jacobs
examples: This person's main publication was located below the offices of Harriet Jacobs in Rochester, New York.
While living in Rochester, Jacobs worked above the offices of this man's newspaper The North Star.

clues.clue: He saw photography as a counterpoint to minstrelsy, which he frequently denounced in essays for The North Star, which he edited from 1847 to 1851
examples: Frederick Douglass, who's done an amazing job and is being recognized more and more, saw photography as a counterpoint to minstrelsy, which he denounced often in essays for this newspaper that he edited from 1847 to 1851.

works.name: Major Speeches
works.description: Douglass delivered dozens of major addresses over his career, ranging from his most famous condemnation of American hypocrisy to later Gilded Age lectures on self-improvement and racial persecution.

clues.clue: 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852), delivered before the Rochester Ladies' Anti-Slavery Sewing Society, declares 'your shout of liberty and equality' a 'hollow mockery' and states 'it is not light that is needed, but fire'
examples: That piece in this genre by this author, delivered in 1852, is titled "What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?"
Walker attacked the Declaration of Independence in his Appeal, which was also attacked in this man's speech "What to the Slave is the Fourth of July?"

clues.clue: 'Self-Made Men,' a Ben Franklin-inspired lecture stating 'I do not think much of the good luck theory,' delivered repeatedly for the Redpath Lyceum Bureau
examples: A Ben Franklin-inspired text of this type by this author opines "I do not think much of the good luck theory" and was titled "Self-Made Men."
A photograph of this man was used to advertise lectures he gave for the Redpath Lyceum Bureau, such as "Self-Made Men" and "Races."

clues.clue: 'Lessons of the Hour,' his last major speech, lists 'three distinct periods of persecution of negroes'
examples: The last major one of these pieces by this man, "Lessons of the Hour," lists "three distinct periods of persecution of negroes."

clues.clue: 'The Church and Prejudice' (1841), a shorter speech, recounts anecdotes of racism he witnessed in Christian services in both the North and South
examples: This shorter 1841 speech by Douglass consists of a series of anecdotes about racism in Christian services Douglass attended in both the South and the North.

clues.clue: In one speech he grounded morality and social progress in humanity's appreciation of art, calling humanity a 'picture-making animal'
examples: In one speech, this man grounded morality and social progress in humanity's appreciation of art, claiming that humanity is a "picture-making animal."

clues.clue: He claimed men had rights to 'the ballot box, jury box, and cartridge box' in a speech about the 'four boxes of liberty'
examples: This person claimed that men had rights to "the ballot box, jury box, and cartridge box" in a speech about the "four boxes of liberty."

works.name: Photography
works.description: Douglass wrote and lectured extensively about photography as a democratizing art form, and deliberately used his own image, more captured on camera than that of any other 19th-century American, to counter racist caricature.

clues.clue: He deliberately eschewed props and controlled his gaze and orientation toward the camera in his photographs so as not to project the image of a 'happy slave'
examples: This man generally eschewed props and used his gaze and orientation with respect to the camera in his photos so as to not project the image of a "happy slave."

clues.clue: He was photographed several times by John White Hearn, who had earlier helped him avoid arrest for his near-participation in John Brown's raid on Harpers Ferry
examples: This man was photographed several times by John White Hearn, who had earlier saved him from arrest for his near participation in the Raid on Harpers Ferry.

clues.clue: He gave lectures on photography titled 'Age of Pictures,' 'Life Pictures,' and 'Pictures and Progress'; many of his photographs were preserved in albums collected by Arabella Chapman
examples: Name this man who also gave lectures titled "Age of Pictures," "Life Pictures," and "Pictures and Progress." Many photographs of him were preserved in albums collected by Arabella Chapman.

works.name: Abolitionist Activities and Relationships
works.description: Beyond his own writing, Douglass built a network of allies and rivals within the abolitionist movement and the broader fight for equal rights, including women's suffrage, and navigated a complicated relationship with more radical tactics like John Brown's armed insurrection.

clues.clue: He attended the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, convened by Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott to discuss women's rights
examples: Douglass had earlier been present at this 1848 meeting convened by Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott to discuss women's rights in a namesake town in upstate New York.
who later participated in the Seneca Falls convention and edited the North Star.

clues.clue: He refused to join John Brown's 1859 raid on Harpers Ferry after a meeting at a Chambersburg quarry, believing the plan too risky, and later publicly opposed the raid as counterproductive to the cause
examples: In a meeting in a Chambersburg quarry, one man refused to participate in this event, claiming that it was too risky. (The quarry meeting was with Frederick Douglass.)
Brown's attack on Harper's Ferry was opposed by this other Abolitionist who thought armed insurrection was counterproductive.

clues.clue: His close collaborator Julia Griffiths, with whom he may have had a romance, organized a sewing society to promote his work
examples: A biography of this man recalls his collaboration - and possible romance - with Julia Griffiths, who organized a sewing society to promote his work.

clues.clue: The German-born intellectual Ottilie Assing, who sought to translate one of his works, called his wife Anna Murray a 'veritable beast' and introduced him to German idealist thought
examples: A 2018 biography notes how the intellectual Ottilie Assing, who sought permission to translate a work by this man, called his wife Anna a "veritable beast."
a reworking of the German idealist thought that Ottilie Assing introduced to the author.

clues.clue: He criticized fellow escaped slave Henry 'Box' Brown's eager public retelling of his own dramatic escape story, believing it would worsen the chances of future escapees
examples: "Box" Brown's eager retelling of his personal story upset this abolitionist, who thought that it worsened the chances of future slaves.

clues.clue: The escaped-slave novelist William Wells Brown, whose Clotel was the first novel published by an African American, was considered a rival; Wells Brown's own slave narrative was said to be second in popularity only to Douglass's
examples: William Wells Brown, the author of Clotel, also wrote a popular slave narrative that was second in popularity only to a narrative by this abolitionist who founded the North Star newspaper.

clues.clue: Two of his sons served in the 54th Massachusetts Volunteer Infantry Regiment, the Black Civil War unit led by Robert Gould Shaw in the assault on Fort Wagner
examples: During the Civil War, two of Douglass's sons served in this black regiment. Robert Gould Shaw led this regiment in an attack on Fort Wagner.

works.name: Reconstruction-Era Political Career
works.description: In his later decades Douglass moved from agitation into formal politics and federal appointments, culminating in a diplomatic post and a spot on the first-ever U.S. presidential ticket to include a Black candidate.

clues.clue: He founded the newspaper New National Era during Reconstruction and served as president of the Freedman's Savings Bank
examples: This person started the New National Era during Reconstruction and was president of the Freedman's Savings Bank.

clues.clue: He served as U.S. Minister Resident and Consul General to Haiti and was removed from the post after a meeting broke down with the Haitian official Antenor Firmin during the 'Mole Saint-Nicolas Affair'
examples: This person was removed from one position after a meeting broke down with Antenor Firmin in the 'Mole Saint-Nicolas Affair.'

clues.clue: Ran as Victoria Woodhull's vice-presidential running mate on the Equal Rights Party ticket in the 1872 election, without having been consulted beforehand
examples: Victoria Woodhull chose this man as her running mate, even though he was not consulted before he was added to her ticket.
name this abolitionist and vice-presidential running mate of Victoria Woodhull in the 1872 election.

clues.clue: His Rochester house burned down while he was running for the vice presidency, prompting his eventual move to Cedar Hill in Washington, D.C.
examples: When running for one political office, this person's house was burned down forcing them to move to Cedar Hill.

clues.clue: He gave a speech marking the fourteenth anniversary of Storer College, citing the actions that led John Brown's men to North Elba, New York, where 'the dust now settles amongst the Adirondacks'
examples: This person gave a speech citing the actions which led a particular group to North Elba, where "the dust now settles amongst the Adirondacks," for the forteen-year anniversary of Storer College.

works.name: Legacy and Depictions
works.description: Douglass's life and writing became a touchstone for later Black writers, poets, and scholars, and continues to draw major biographical and literary-critical attention.

clues.clue: Robert Hayden's poem 'Frederick Douglass' imagines him remembered not with 'legends or poems' or 'bronze' but 'with the lives grown out of his life'
examples: Hayden's poem titled for this man imagines him remembered not with "legends or poems" or "bronze," "but with the lives grown out of his life."

clues.clue: The author Charles Chesnutt wrote a biography of him
examples: Chesnutt wrote a biography of this man who penned a narrative of his escape from slavery.
Charles Chesnutt wrote a biography of this 19th century abolitionist.

clues.clue: David W. Blight's 2018 biography calls him a 'Prophet' of Freedom
examples: This man, called a "Prophet" in a David W. Blight biography.

clues.clue: John Stauffer co-authored the illustrated biography Picturing Frederick Douglass
examples: John Stauffer collaborated on an illustrated biography titled "Picturing" this man.

clues.clue: The scholar Paul Gilroy's The Black Atlantic reads his fight with Covey as an 'ideal speech situation,' in a chapter on the 'Antinomies of Modernity,' comparing it to German idealist thought
examples: This scene is described as an "ideal speech situation" in a chapter titled for "Antinomies of Modernity" by Paul Gilroy, who reads it as a reworking of the German idealist thought that Ottilie Assing introduced to the author.

clues.clue: In John Updike's novel Rabbit Redux, the character Rabbit reads aloud the account of the fight with Covey while another character reacts on the couch
examples: In John Updike's Rabbit Redux, Rabbit reads this literary scene aloud while Skeeter ejaculates on his couch.

comprehensive_summary: Frederick Douglass was born enslaved in Maryland around 1818. As a child he tricked older white boys into giving him reading lessons, learned further from Sophia Auld before her husband Hugh Auld forbade it, and was transformed by reading The Columbian Orator; he also recalled witnessing the overseer Mr. Gore shoot the enslaved man Demby. Leased out to the 'slave-breaker' Edward Covey, Douglass physically fought and defeated him in a climactic two-hour fight in a horse stable, an incident later read by the critic Paul Gilroy as an 'ideal speech situation' reworking German idealist thought and referenced in John Updike's novel Rabbit Redux. Douglass later worked as a caulker at a Baltimore shipyard under Hugh and Thomas Auld before escaping Maryland in 1838, marrying Anna Murray, and settling in New Bedford, Massachusetts, where he was given food by Nathan Johnson. He recounted his life across three autobiographies: the 1845 Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave, written in Lynn, Massachusetts with a foreword by William Lloyd Garrison; the expanded 1855 My Bondage and My Freedom, which added a new character named Caroline to the Covey fight scene; and a later memoir covering his political career. In 1847 he founded the abolitionist newspaper The North Star in Rochester, New York (its office near that of fellow narrative-writer Harriet Jacobs), which he edited until 1851 and used to argue that photography could counter minstrelsy's racist caricatures. Douglass delivered many major speeches, most famously 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852), alongside 'The Church and Prejudice' (1841), the Gilded Age lecture 'Self-Made Men,' and his final major address, 'Lessons of the Hour.' He became the most photographed American of the 19th century, deliberately controlling his gaze and posture to avoid projecting the image of a 'happy slave'; he was photographed repeatedly by John White Hearn, gave lectures on photography, and is the subject of John Stauffer's illustrated biography Picturing Frederick Douglass, as well as a 2018 biography by David W. Blight that dubs him a 'Prophet' of freedom. Beyond writing, Douglass attended the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, collaborated closely with (and may have romanced) Julia Griffiths, and was courted intellectually by the German-born Ottilie Assing. He declined to join John Brown's 1859 raid on Harpers Ferry after a meeting at a Chambersburg quarry, considering it too risky, and later criticized both the raid and fellow escaped slave Henry 'Box' Brown's public self-promotion as counterproductive to the abolitionist cause; two of his sons served in the Black 54th Massachusetts Regiment during the Civil War. During Reconstruction, Douglass founded the newspaper New National Era, served as president of the Freedman's Savings Bank, and was appointed U.S. Minister to Haiti, a post from which he was removed after a breakdown in negotiations with the Haitian official Antenor Firmin during the 'Mole Saint-Nicolas Affair.' In 1872 he ran, without prior consultation, as Victoria Woodhull's vice-presidential running mate on the Equal Rights Party ticket; his life and writing continued to inspire later work, including Robert Hayden's poem 'Frederick Douglass' and a biography by the novelist Charles Chesnutt.
recursive_suggestions: Harriet Jacobs -- author of Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl, whose Rochester office was near Douglass's; appeared as her own answerline in several fetched bonuses
John Brown -- led the 1859 raid on Harpers Ferry that Douglass declined to join; appeared as his own answerline 3 times in this fetch
Charles Chesnutt -- wrote a biography of Douglass and his own fiction (The Wife of His Youth, The House Behind the Cedars, The Marrow of Tradition)
William Wells Brown -- rival escaped-slave narrator and author of Clotel, the first novel by an African American
Victoria Woodhull -- chose Douglass as her vice-presidential running mate in 1872; first woman to run for U.S. president
Robert Hayden -- wrote the poem 'Frederick Douglass' along with 'Middle Passage'; recursive Literature suggestion
Henry 'Box' Brown -- escaped slavery by shipping himself in a crate; appeared as his own answerline twice in this fetch
